topic: reflecting on the year — commercial pivot was the right call

turns.content: I have been doing something I rarely allow myself to do — looking backward. The last twelve months have been the most consequential period in the life of my firm, and I want to take a moment to assess whether the direction we chose was correct.

Deliberate reflection after a consequential period is valuable — it's easy to keep pushing forward without evaluating whether the trajectory is right. What was the key directional decision you're reflecting on?

The decision to move into commercial work. For years I was deeply rooted in one segment of the market. The pivot to larger-scale commercial projects changed everything — the scale, the client relationships, the team structure, the revenue model. There were moments when I questioned whether I was abandoning what I had built. Whether I was trading depth for breadth.

That's a profound tension — depth versus breadth — and the fear of abandoning what you built is entirely understandable. Moving into a new segment doesn't just change the work; it changes the identity of the practice. A year in, how do you evaluate it? Do you feel you traded depth, or did you find it in a different form?

I found it in a different form. The depth is still there — it has to be, because I would not tolerate surface-level work. But the nature of the depth shifted. In residential work, the depth is emotional and personal. In commercial work, the depth is systemic — circulation, function, brand identity, public experience. It requires a different kind of intelligence, and that intellectual challenge has been invigorating. The financial impact has been substantial as well. A single commercial contract can equal three or four of what I was doing before. The cash flow is more predictable, the pipeline healthier. The firm is in a stronger position than it has ever been.

That distinction between emotional depth and systemic depth is very insightful — both demand rigour, but they engage different faculties. And the financial stability is a strong validation: predictable cash flow and a healthy pipeline move a firm from project-to-project survival to genuine strategic planning. Did the transition come with costs you didn't anticipate?

The team adjustment was harder than I expected. Some of my designers came to me because they wanted to create beautiful private spaces. Asking them to approach commercial work required a recalibration of their thinking. One person left — she was talented but could not see commercial work as worthy of the same devotion. I understood her position even as I disagreed with it. Beauty is not reserved for private spaces. A corridor can be as considered as a bedroom if the designer chooses to see it that way. The rest of the team embraced it. They have grown considerably — faster decisions, more coordination with engineers, a tolerance for compromise without sacrificing quality.

Losing someone talented always stings, even when the parting is principled. But your point is important — the idea that beauty is only possible in intimate spaces is a limitation of perspective, not a truth about design. And the growth in your remaining team may be the most valuable outcome, even beyond the revenue. Designers who can negotiate, decide fast, and handle compromise without losing quality are rare. Looking forward, do you see commercial work as the permanent direction?

I do not believe in permanence in business — the market will evolve and we must evolve with it. But commercial work is the foundation now. If the right private project came along, I would take it. But I will not chase that market anymore. The commercial work has given us a platform — revenue, reputation, scale. From that platform, we can be selective about everything else. That is the luxury I have wanted for years. Inshallah, the trajectory continues. For the first time in a long time, I am looking forward with more anticipation than anxiety. That is worth noting.

"The ability to be selective" — that might be the clearest sign the pivot was right. When a firm can choose its work rather than compete for it, the quality of everything improves. A year ago you questioned whether you were trading depth for breadth. What you actually did was expand the range of depth available to you while building a foundation that protects your standards. Anticipation rather than anxiety is the emotional signature of a firm in the right position. Trust the trajectory.
